one_line_takeaway: A CLTC/Berkeley policy white paper that critiques existing industry AI-cyber risk thresholds as vague and non-probabilistic, and proposes (without building or validating) Bayesian networks as the modeling framework to make such thresholds evidence-based and operationalizable, illustrated only qualitatively via an AI-augmented-phishing decomposition.
key_finding: The paper's core claims are argumentative/normative, not empirical results it produced: that (1) current industry cyber thresholds are methodologically weak because they rely on deterministic capability cutoffs, vague qualifiers, and comparisons to prior AI systems rather than human/non-AI baselines; and (2) Bayesian networks are theoretically well suited to fill this gap because they handle data scarcity, represent uncertainty probabilistically, and support diagnostic, sensitivity, and scenario analysis -- but the paper explicitly stops short of building, parameterizing, or validating an actual BN, and states this as future work ('More research is needed to create and test the processes needed to systematically construct a BN'). All quantitative figures appearing in the case study (phishing click-through rates, detection accuracies, attack-volume growth) are pulled from cited third-party studies to illustrate what kind of evidence *could* populate BN nodes -- they are not results the CLTC authors measured or validated themselves.
core_contribution: This is a CLTC (UC Berkeley Center for Long-Term Cybersecurity) policy/methodology white paper, not an empirical ML paper. It makes three explicit contributions, building on the authors' prior work (Raman et al., 2025): (1) a survey and critique of existing industry AI-cyber risk thresholds (Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta, OpenAI, Amazon, Microsoft, G42, Magic, xAI), synthesized into a comparison table of recurring 'threshold elements' (threat-actor skill level, novel strategy development, exploit/zero-day development, infrastructure targeting, multi-stage operations, complete attack-chain automation) and five identified methodological shortcomings (overfocus on novel/low-probability threats, reliance on deterministic yes/no cutoffs instead of probabilistic measures, vague qualifiers like 'meaningful' or 'significant', lack of clear non-AI/human comparison baselines, and imprecise scope of what is being assessed); (2) a proposal to use Bayesian networks (BNs) as the modeling tool for AI-enabled cyber risk thresholds, described at the level of network-construction methodology (defining nodes/states, structure/edges, parameterization via expert elicitation plus EM-algorithm updating, belief propagation, and a proposed 7-test psychometric validation framework adapted from Pitchforth & Mengersen 2013 -- nomological, face, content, concurrent, predictive validity, etc.); and (3) an illustrative, non-quantified worked example applying this decomposition to one threat -- AI-augmented phishing -- showing how a threshold statement can be broken into sub-nodes (Vulnerability Discovery, Credential Theft & Abuse, Exploit Development, Reconnaissance, Social Engineering, Augmenting Code and Processes; and within Social Engineering: Lure Credibility, AI Linguistic Mastery, Threat-Actor Profile, Defense Detection Rate, Defender Adaptation Speed, Deepfakes) and populated with candidate qualitative/quantitative evidence types (Table 4, Table 5). No actual Bayesian network is built, parameterized, or run in this paper -- Figures 1-3 are illustrative diagrams of node/edge structure, not a computed or evaluated model. The paper's own contribution is explicitly methodological/conceptual: 'The key contribution of this paper is not the threshold statement itself, but rather the methodology that operationalizes it.'
threat_model: The paper's threat model is broad and taxonomic rather than a single formalized attack scenario: AI systems (frontier/general-purpose generative AI, including LLMs, multimodal models, and agents) lowering the expertise, cost, and time barriers for cyber operations across the kill chain -- reconnaissance, vulnerability discovery, exploit development, credential theft, and especially AI-augmented social engineering/phishing (the paper's illustrative case study). It motivates this with real-world incidents cited from other sources: an early-2025 'vibe hacking' data-extortion campaign across 17 organizations by a low-skill actor using an AI coding agent (Anthropic, 2025d) and a November 2025 GTG-1002 Chinese state-sponsored campaign in which AI autonomously executed 80-90% of tactical operations (Anthropic reporting). These are reported incidents cited from Anthropic's threat-intelligence disclosures, not something the CLTC authors investigated or verified themselves.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) no BN is actually constructed, parameterized, or validated in this paper -- it is a methodological roadmap, and the authors state 'the use of BNs in AI risk management remains underexplored' and identify multiple open research gaps; (2) BN practical implementations (per cited Barrett et al. 2025) often rely on strong structural simplifications, e.g. binary success/failure nodes, that omit partial successes and interaction effects; (3) validating BNs for intolerable-risk thresholds is hard because 'objective datasets for intolerable risk thresholds do not yet exist'; (4) BNs require ongoing structural (not just parameter) maintenance to avoid becoming obsolete as the threat landscape shifts, which is non-trivial and costly; (5) inputs used to parameterize nodes are subject to bias, e.g., industry threat reports may overstate specific threats (like phishing) to drive market demand; (6) even a well-parameterized BN cannot resolve where the 'intolerable' risk line is drawn, since that is characterized as a societal rather than statistical judgment, with slow feedback loops for involuntary/diffuse risks. As a reviewer note: because the paper synthesizes numbers from many disparate third-party sources of varying rigor and methodology (academic proof-of-concept studies, industry telemetry reports, vendor red-teaming write-ups) without independently verifying them, the phishing case-study numbers should be read as illustrative inputs, not validated ground truth.

quantitative_results: Cited (not authors' own): APWG reported 1,130,393 phishing attacks in Q2 2025 vs. 274,681 in Q2 2020, a 311% increase (APWG 2025; APWG 2020).
Cited (not authors' own): FBI IC3 2024 Annual Report -- phishing/spoofing was the highest-complaint-count crime type, with 193,407 complaints and over $70 million in reported losses (FBI, 2024b).

Cited (not authors' own): Proofpoint 2024 -- over 70% of organizations experienced bulk phishing/spear phishing/BEC/smishing/social-media scams in 2023; over 30% of successful attacks led to data/IP loss or ransomware infection.
Cited (not authors' own): DSIT UK 2024 survey -- 84% of UK businesses and 83% of charities reported phishing attacks in 2024.

Cited (not authors' own): Seymour & Tully (2018) automated spear-phishing tool achieved a 30-66% click-through rate vs. traditional attacks, using K-means clustering plus LSTM for target/content generation.

Cited (not authors' own): Heiding et al. (2024a) OSINT-automated reconnaissance/outreach phishing proof-of-concept achieved a 54% click-through rate, comparable to human experts, vs. 12% for traditional (non-personalized) attacks; the same study reported Claude 3.5 Sonnet achieved 97.25% true-positive phishing-detection rate with zero false positives.
Cited (not authors' own): Lohn & Jackson (2022) prior modeling work -- optimistic-capability scenarios put AI phishing on par with human experts at a 30% click-through rate.

Cited (not authors' own): Begou et al. (2023) -- an LLM-driven phishing-page-cloning pipeline took ~29 seconds per page on average and succeeded 31.25% of the time, limited in part by GPT-3.5-turbo-16K's 16,384-token context window (contrasted by the CLTC authors with GPT-5's 400,000-token window, ~24x larger).
Cited (not authors' own): Roy et al. (2024) -- a BERT-based malicious-prompt detector achieved 94% average accuracy for phishing-email prompts and 96% for phishing-website prompts.

Cited (not authors' own): Sahingoz et al. (2024) -- deep-learning phishing defenses achieved up to 98.9% accuracy; Al-Ahmadi (2022)/Kavya & Sumathi (2024) -- GAN-based PDGAN system reached 97.6% accuracy.

Cited (not authors' own): Magic AGI Readiness Policy defines its cyber threshold quantitatively as at least a 10x reduction in cost of discovering new zero-days/breaking critical infrastructure for a malicious expert actor.
Cited (not authors' own): xAI defines 'catastrophic malicious use events' as thresholds of over 100 deaths or $1 billion in damages.
baselines_compared: Not an experimental paper with author-run baselines. The paper instead compares Bayesian networks qualitatively against three alternative risk-assessment methodologies: qualitative/heatmap risk labeling, fault tree analysis, and Monte Carlo simulation, arguing BNs better handle data scarcity, probabilistic uncertainty, and complex interaction/mitigation modeling.

It also tabulates and compares industry frontier-safety-framework cyber thresholds across nine organizations (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Amazon, Microsoft, G42, Magic, Meta, xAI) as a structural/qualitative comparison, not a quantitative benchmark evaluation.
